reasoning:
The 360-day average of 'Protests' in Senegal is 8.167 per 30-day period. Because event counts are positively skewed, the median is generally lower than the mean, making the base-rate probability of exceeding the mean in a typical month less than 50% (around 40%).

For the first resolution date (2026-02-22), the 30-day window spans Jan 23 to Feb 21. Recent news confirms that Senegal has been experiencing "weeks of unrest" culminating in significant student protests over delayed stipends at the Cheikh Anta Diop University (UCAD) in Dakar. This reached a boiling point on Feb 10, when clashes resulted in the death of a student, Abdoulaye Ba, drawing thousands into protests. These continuous demonstrations and the immediate subsequent outrage will very likely generate more than the 8.167 required ACLED "Protest" events for this window. Hence, I assign a high probability (75%).

For the second resolution date (2026-03-17), the 30-day window is from Feb 15 to Mar 16. Because UCAD was officially closed on Feb 11 and students were evacuated and instructed by groups to 'stay at home', the epicenter of the protests has been dissolved. Furthermore, the peak protests reacting to the Feb 10 death likely occurred before Feb 15, excluding them from this window. Without the centralized mass of students, protest frequency is expected to drop back toward or below the historical mean. Reflecting this cooling effect and the increased uncertainty of a longer forecast horizon, my estimate falls to 48%, slightly below 50% in alignment with the naturally skewed distribution of such events.
